title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bacon and Craddock discuss the possibility that witnesses may be withholding information. They are interrupted by Old Crickenshaw, the owner of the house where the murder occurred. Crickenshaw notes that the murder took place in his Elizabethan warehouse. He speculates that the victim was a foreigner or a spy sent to steal industrial secrets from the nearby metal box factory. He further suggests the woman was connected to his son, Alfred, claiming a brute killed her while she was attempting to meet him. Craddock counters that Alfred did not recognize the woman. After Crickenshaw departs, Bacon dismisses the theory about Alfred, suggesting instead that they focus on Brian Estridge, an aimless former Air Force veteran. Bacon also questions the logic of the killer burying the victim in a coffin within the victim's own father-in-law's house.

Craddock then visits Miss Marple. Craddock explains that he was brought onto the case after the Deputy Commissioner became interested in her involvement. They discuss the investigation, which began after Mrs. McGillicuddy reported the case. Miss Marple explains that her insight comes from her deep knowledge of Mrs. McGillicuddy, noting that the woman would not have hallucinated the situation. Miss Marple posits that the killer must be at or very familiar with Larchfield Manor. Craddock notes that this includes many people, such as former maids or locals. Finally, they discuss the difficulty of identifying the victim. Forensic evidence suggests she was approximately thirty-five years old, healthy, and possibly married with a child. Her fur coat was purchased in London around Christmas Eve, while her other clothing was from Paris. Police are currently checking missing persons reports in both London and Paris.
